New Star Trek RPG coming

  • Thread starter TristramEvans
  • Start date Apr 26, 2017
  • Tags
    modiphius prime directive star trek
  • 1
  • 2
  • 3
Next
1 of 3

Go to page

Next Last

TristramEvans

The Right Hand of Doom
Administrator
Moderator
Joined
Apr 24, 2017
Messages
40,398
Reaction score
124,996
  • Apr 26, 2017
  • #1
From Modiphius, apparently (who also now how the license for a Warhammer RPG).

Modiphius said:
We'll be launching pre-orders for Star Trek Adventures in May and will give you a heads up so you know when it's coming. Here's a sneak peak of what you can expect: Two core books - the 1701-D Limited Edition and the art cover Starfleet Edition, Q-Workshop dice sets for each division, Mission Book, sets of 8 high quality 32mm scale miniatures including The Original Series Enterprise Crew, The Next Generation Series Enterprise Crew and Away Team, Romulan and Klingon crews with lots more in development. Ship deck plans and two incredible Collector's Edition box sets that we think you'll love. Beyond the launch there will be Division and Quadrant supplements, Campaigns, more deck plans and miniatures. We'll be unveiling images of these in the coming weeks!
Click to expand...

I'm not familiar with Modiphius at all, other than the recent thread on Conan games.

Are they going to try and squeeze Star Trek into their 2d20 engine like they did with Conan? I wasn't too impressed with what I saw there.

36,483
  • Apr 26, 2017
  • #4
Endless Flight said:
Are they going to try and squeeze Star Trek into their 2d20 engine like they did with Conan? I wasn't too impressed with what I saw there.
Click to expand...
Yes. Nathan Dowdell had some kind of design epiphany while playing Cortex+ games, so Modiphius is basically using the Cortex+ rulebook. Develop a highly narrative RPG with flexible rules (although not too flexible in dropping the narrative elements), buy a ton of licenses and adjust the rules to fit the setting/genre.

The Decipher version was the last version.
However, Amarillo Design Bureau is supposedly using Mongoose Traveller I to make an RPG in the Star Fleet Battles Universe.
 
Last edited: Apr 26, 2017

Spinachcat

Legendary Pubber
Joined
Apr 27, 2017
Messages
762
Reaction score
1,154
  • Apr 28, 2017
  • #8
I joined the Star Trek playtest, but my crew isn't jazzed to even read the rules. I am thinking about doing a one shot to see how it works for ST. If I do, I will post some info.

If ADB is doing a SFB RPG, that would be interesting. I ran Star Fleet Battles Traveller back in the day and it works great IF you are cool with TPKs happening. SFB isn't ST. It's ST at war 24/7 and like the Kreator song says "It's everybody against everybody against everybody".

While that multi-empires at war gives lots of battle options for the SFB minis game, it makes it uber dangerous as a RPG and some ST players get a tad shocked with the game ends because they got blasted to bits.
 

Endless Flight

Dog Dad
Administrator
Moderator

Messages
17,385
Reaction score
48,411
  • Apr 28, 2017
  • #9
Who owns the Star Trek property rights? Is it a movie studio or another company? I'm curious if it's similar to the Lord of the Rings where you have the rights spread around.
 

TristramEvans

The Right Hand of Doom
Administrator
Moderator
Joined
Apr 24, 2017
Messages
40,398
Reaction score
124,996
  • Apr 28, 2017
  • #10
Paramount owns all current rights, but there's a special exception for Star Fleet Battles, in that they have the license for games in perpetuity, but this was before TNG, so they can only do variations and extensions of TOS.

Also, there is free PDF for their Prime Directive RPG

I heard the story a long time ago, but dont have any current links. Is the Prime Directive RPG GURPs-based or was that something different?
 

CRKrueger

Eläytyminatör
Joined
Apr 25, 2017
Messages
14,645
Reaction score
36,483
  • Apr 29, 2017
  • #13
The original Prime Directive used its own system. The also, over the years, have done versions in GURPS, D20, and D20 Modern.

The idea is that the PCs are members of a Prime Team, a dedicated Away Team of specialists to conduct missions, tossing away the "send the entire crop of bridge officers and department heads down to planets leaving the ship without primary command and control" aspect of the various Star Trek series.

https://www.modiphius.net/collections/star-trek-adventures/products/star-trek-adventures-starter-set
A starter set for under $30. This seems like a great idea and high value. Three adventures, pre-gens of crew and ship, dice, and cardboard tokens. Must. Resist. Urge. To. Buy.

Resistance is futile.



(Also, I necroed the oldest thread again. )

Dumarest

Vaquero de Alta California
Joined
Jan 20, 2018
Messages
15,714
Reaction score
34,730
  • May 16, 2018
  • #22
Spinachcat said:
The ADB "ownership" of Star Fleet Battles is a weird thing. Anyone know the real scoop?
Click to expand...

They licensed Franz Joseph's Technical Manual work which was separately licensed by Gene Roddenberry back in the '70s and is technically a separate thing from regular Star Trek and now considered non-"canon." They have no license to any other material. That's why they only use material from the original show and nothing from later films or shows.
 
Reactions: The Butcher

  • May 16, 2018
  • #25
I played in a game of this at a con earlier in the year. It had a very nice ST flavour during the first, investigative part of the game but fell apart in the second half with a very ponderous ship to ship conflict. First part was great though. The system did little to add much to the game but it didn't get in the way either; it was fairly light in nature which is not the same for all the 2d20 games. I would certainly say it was more TNG in feel than OST. I'd try it again if the s-t-s was kept away.
 
Reactions: Charlie D and Voros

Lunar Ronin

New Generation Grognard
Administrator
Joined
Jul 29, 2017
Messages
3,749
Reaction score
8,964
  • May 16, 2018
  • #26
TristramEvans said:
It looks surprisingly like TNG era instead of new films?
Click to expand...

As I broke down in the Discovery thread, Star Trek is now co-owned by two entirely separate companies, CBS and Viacom. CBS owns the rights to Star Trek television. Viacom owns the rights to Star Trek movies. It breaks down further because Viacom and J.J. Abrams' Bad Robot co-own the Kelvin Timeline Star Trek movies. Because of this, Star Trek television shows can not reference at all the Kelvin Timeline nor events from the past three films (which is why Discovery is set in the Prime Timeline), and the Star Trek movies can't directly reference the television shows. It's a mess. A mess that just got a whole lot messier because CBS sued Viacom last week, putting all Star Trek projects on hold, including three television movies Nicholas Meyer has been working on.

Anyway, Viacom and Bad Robot are a lot more judicious with licensing out Star Trek than CBS. Modiphius has the license with CBS, not Viacom. So Star Trek Adventures can not legally mention the Kelvin Timeline nor use anything from the past three movies.
 
Reactions: Spinachcat, Nick J, TristramEvans and 1 other person

As I broke down in the Discovery thread, Star Trek is now co-owned by two entirely separate companies, CBS and Viacom. CBS owns the rights to Star Trek television. Viacom owns the rights to Star Trek movies. It breaks down further because Viacom and J.J. Abrams' Bad Robot co-own the Kelvin Timeline Star Trek movies. Because of this, Star Trek television shows can not reference at all the Kelvin Timeline nor events from the past three films (which is why Discovery is set in the Prime Timeline), and the Star Trek movies can't directly reference the television shows. It's a mess. A mess that just got a whole lot messier because CBS sued Viacom last week, putting all Star Trek projects on hold, including three television movies Nicholas Meyer has been working on.

Anyway, Viacom and Bad Robot are a lot more judicious with licensing out Star Trek than CBS. Modiphius has the license with CBS, not Viacom. So Star Trek Adventures can not legally mention the Kelvin Timeline nor use anything from the past three movies.
Click to expand...

Wow. LOL, seems like you could take a metaphor of that and turn it into a good plot for the show itself...

I don't mind the 2d20 system but it seems silly to apply it to so many different settings (Conan, John Carter of Mars and Star Trek?), I prefer when the system is built from the ground up to fit the setting.

They take an extreme level of tailoring the system to the setting. Mutant Chronicles resembles the old Mutant Chronicles system in many ways and Star Trek doesn't at all. I have mixed feelings. OD&D is too narrativist for my tastes...
 
Reactions: The Butcher

Harl Quinn

Veteran of the Clone Wars
Joined
May 12, 2017
Messages
606
Reaction score
904
  • May 17, 2018
  • #30
Voros said:
I don't mind the 2d20 system but it seems silly to apply it to so many different settings (Conan, John Carter of Mars and Star Trek?), I prefer when the system is built from the ground up to fit the setting.
Click to expand...
I agree with you regarding the Star Trek system. I own both the old FASA Star Trek RPG (2d10, percentile skills, etc.) and the version produced by Last Unicorn Games (Icon system). I've heard Decipher's Coda system is similar to Icon, but never bought the books as I didn't have the time and money to invest in them.

Looking at the 2d20 system for Star Trek, I was not impressed. I got the feeling they were going the route of CP2020 where every role has its special gift (Authority for Cops in CP2020, the ability to work engineering miracles in Star Trek, for example) - a cookie cutter with some minimal variations, in a sense. I'm happy Star Trek is still in the RPG sphere, but it definitely deserves a better system.

Not to derail, but for me, system does matter. For example, I prefer the original D6 Star Wars RPG to the other incarnations (d20, Saga, FFG's Genesys). For me, having grown up with Star Wars, I can pretty well wing things and keep the power levels tamped down or ramped up as necessary. The initial appeal I found in the game was the fact that it was a well known license from my childhood set to rules using common dice you can steal from a boardgame if necessary.
 
Reactions: Endless Flight and Ragr

Harl Quinn said:
Looking at the 2d20 system for Star Trek, I was not impressed. I got the feeling they were going the route of CP2020 where every role has its special gift (Authority for Cops in CP2020, the ability to work engineering miracles in Star Trek, for example) - a cookie cutter with some minimal variations, in a sense. I'm happy Star Trek is still in the RPG sphere, but it definitely deserves a better system.
Click to expand...

Not that there's anything wrong with Cyberpunk. It's great at what it does, which is action movie roleplaying.

Personally, I'm not convinced Star Trek is a good setting for RPGs. Too many plots are resolved with a technobabble asspull. In other words, someone spouts some gobbledegook and the reset button gets hit just in time for next week. It makes for a difficult RPG to moderate.
 
Reactions: Picaroon Jack and Baulderstone

Dumarest

Vaquero de Alta California
Joined
Jan 20, 2018
Messages
15,714
Reaction score
34,730
  • May 17, 2018
  • #32
Stevethulhu said:
Not that there's anything wrong with Cyberpunk. It's great at what it does, which is action movie roleplaying.

Personally, I'm not convinced Star Trek is a good setting for RPGs. Too many plots are resolved with a technobabble asspull. In other words, someone spouts some gobbledegook and the reset button gets hit just in time for next week. It makes for a difficult RPG to moderate.
Click to expand...
I think you're conflating attempting to emulate TV plots with playing out a roleplaying scenario. I've had many successful sessions of FASA Trek, none of which resorted to what you describe. I see the same error made with people trying to emulate comic books instead of playing superhero RPGs. Emulating fiction doesn't really work as a game as fiction has predetermined outcomes and characters do whatever the plot requires.
 
Reactions: Picaroon Jack

Baulderstone

Legendary Pubber
Joined
May 13, 2017
Messages
12,614
Reaction score
41,463
  • May 17, 2018
  • #33
Stevethulhu said:
Not that there's anything wrong with Cyberpunk. It's great at what it does, which is action movie roleplaying.

Personally, I'm not convinced Star Trek is a good setting for RPGs. Too many plots are resolved with a technobabble asspull. In other words, someone spouts some gobbledegook and the reset button gets hit just in time for next week. It makes for a difficult RPG to moderate.
Click to expand...
There is some truth to that. It's definitely a setting that calls for meta-mechanics if you want to make it feel like an episode of the show. That way a player can invent their own technobabble and then roll or spent currency to make their idea true. Modiphius at least seemed to get that.

When I read the playtest version, I actually liked the game for the first couple of pages. It seemed like a stripped down version of Cortex+. Then I kept reading, and the game kept piling more and more onto the core system, and it started to feel like a heavier, less intuitive, version of Cortex+, which is not something I wanted at all.

Ashen Stars remains my favorite game for Star Trek-like play. It uses Gumshoe, and rather than deal with a complex role to make up some technobabble, I can just make an investigative spend from the appropriate skill. It's quick and easy, and the limited pool means you have to ration your technobabble interventions.
 
Reactions: Voros and Ragr

Charlie D

Man on the Silver Mountain
Joined
Aug 28, 2017

1,849
Reaction score
3,705
  • May 17, 2018
  • #34
Dumarest said:
I think you're conflating attempting to emulate TV plots with playing out a roleplaying scenario. I've had many successful sessions of FASA Trek, none of which resorted to what you describe. I see the same error made with people trying to emulate comic books instead of playing superhero RPGs. Emulating fiction doesn't really work as a game as fiction has predetermined outcomes and characters do whatever the plot requires.
Click to expand...

I don't necessarily disagree but there is a whole approach to playing the new Star Trek as actual episodes and even going so far as to have an opening scene with the family watching the show before switching to the first scene of the episode (game). Runeslinger on YouTube has had great success with this approach and has the family watching as meta-NPCs. About two sessions equal an episode. I don't get it but it really works for him and his group. The Complex Games Apologist is also running the new Star Trek. He concentrates more on the push and pull of Momentum versus Threat in the game.

The rules support how the shows work in some ways. NPCs are brought forward as needed by the players. NPCs gain more and more game stats and abilities the more often they get called forward. They can even become PCs eventually. This models characters like Worf and Chief O'Brien.

Also, there is another YouTube group of gamers who really try to play RPGs rules as written. To really get what the designers intended and/or to try to emulate a genre. It blows my mind, but it does work for some GMs and players.
 
Last edited: May 17, 2018
Reactions: Voros

Baulderstone

Legendary Pubber
Joined
May 13, 2017

12,614
Reaction score
41,463
  • May 17, 2018
  • #35
Dumarest said:
I think you're conflating attempting to emulate TV plots with playing out a roleplaying scenario. I've had many successful sessions of FASA Trek, none of which resorted to what you describe. I see the same error made with people trying to emulate comic books instead of playing superhero RPGs. Emulating fiction doesn't really work as a game as fiction has predetermined outcomes and characters do whatever the plot requires.
Click to expand...
I see plot emulation as something entirely different. There are games that attempt to give a session a structure that adheres to a type of plot, but that isn't the issue here. The issue is that Star Trek characters often do things like have their starship accomplish things a starship usually can't do, and then never do it again. That doesn't dictate a particular plot or outcome. If anything the GM needs to be more on their toes than ever.

  • May 17, 2018

Baulderstone said:
I see plot emulation as something entirely different. There are games that attempt to give a session a structure that adheres to a type of plot, but that isn't the issue here. The issue is that Star Trek characters often do things like have their starship accomplish things a starship usually can't do, and then never do it again.
Click to expand...

That's what they do on the TV shows and in the movies. Which is what you're trying to emulate if you play it that way.
 

Baulderstone

Legendary Pubber
Joined
May 13, 2017
Messages
12,614
Reaction score
41,463
  • May 17, 2018
  • #37
Dumarest said:
That's what they do on the TV shows and in the movies. Which is what you're trying to emulate if you play it that way.
Click to expand...

I was referring to the this comment by you, "I think you're conflating attempting to emulate TV plots with playing out a roleplaying scenario."

Emulating plots involves trying to structure a session so that its plot resembles an episode, that is emulating a plot. As an example, if a game dictates having a cold open before the credits, an A and B plot, and a philosophical rumination about their adventure at the end, it is emulating plot.

Allowing characters to justify actions with technobabble may be meta, but it doesn't dictate any kind of plot. If anything, it can send the plot in directions that the GM didn't anticipate.

Plot emulation and meta-mechanics are different things, and a lot of arguments on the Internet involve people talking past each on it. The old Ghostbusters RPG had meta-mechanics for the players using science to invent technobabble solutions, but it didn't have any kind of narrative plot steering mechanics.

I see plot emulation mechanics and meta-technobabble mechanics as polar opposites. Plot emulation mechanics constrain players and meta-technobabble allows players more options. That doesn't make either thing better or worse than the other, but they are very different things.
 

Dumarest
